answer: A FileHandle object will try to automatically close the file descriptor and emit a process warning if it is not closed using the filehandle.close() method. However, this behavior can be unreliable and the file may not be closed. Node.js may change this behavior in the future, so it is recommended to always explicitly close FileHandles.
The callback-based versions of the node:fs module APIs are preferable over the use of the promise APIs when maximal performance (both in terms of execution time and memory allocation) is required.

filehandle.datasync() forces all currently queued I/O operations associated with the file to the operating system's synchronized I/O completion state, but does not flush modified metadata. filehandle.sync() requests that all data for the open file descriptor is flushed to the storage device, including metadata. You would use filehandle.datasync() when you want to ensure that file data is written to disk but do not need to update metadata, and filehandle.sync() when you want both data and metadata to be flushed.

If the FileHandle points to a character device that only supports blocking reads (such as keyboard or sound card), read operations do not finish until data is available. This can prevent the process from exiting and the stream from closing naturally. To mitigate this, artificially marking end-of-stream by pushing null to the stream and calling stream.read(0) allows the stream to close.

filehandle.pull() is the asynchronous version that returns an async iterable, performing reads in chunkSize-byte chunks (default 128 KB) off the main thread. It supports transforms, options like autoClose, start, limit, and chunkSize, and can be aborted with an AbortSignal. The file handle is locked while the iterable is being consumed and unlocked when iteration completes, an error occurs, or the consumer breaks.

filehandle.pullSync() is the synchronous counterpart, returning a sync iterable that reads the file using synchronous I/O on the main thread. It does not support AbortSignal, and the file handle is locked while the iterable is being consumed.

Use filehandle.pull() when you want non-blocking, asynchronous file reading, suitable for I/O-bound operations or when you want to avoid blocking the event loop. Use filehandle.pullSync() when you need synchronous, blocking file reading, such as in scripts or environments where async code is not practical. Both require the --experimental-stream-iter flag to be enabled.
